title: The Survival Function of Platform Integrity
desc: A Spark-scale Trust & Safety analytics engine built around a cross-domain reframe: the Kaplan-Meier estimator that biostatisticians use to model patient survival is the right tool to model how long a user session stays free of policy-violative content. The engine layers survival analysis, LDA topic modeling, VADER sentiment, and NetworkX co-occurrence into a four-module pipeline (orchestrator, text NLP, statistical, report generator) running on roughly 3,000 cores. The contribution is the methodological import (biostatistics to platform integrity) and the structured 12-section auto-report that makes investigations reproducible across cases.
visual: <svg fill="none" viewBox="0 0 600 600"><circle cx="300" cy="300" r="150" stroke="#3866A0" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="300" cy="300" r="50" fill="#7A1626"/></svg>

title: An ADOS-2-Aligned Multimodal Architecture for Clinical Autism Assessment
desc: The ADOS-2 scores social affect, communication, and repetitive behaviors as separate domains before deriving a composite, and a model that flattens those features into one feature space is doing something the diagnostic algorithm is built around not doing. This pipeline takes the opposite approach: three domain-specific classifiers train independently on social, communication, and repetitive feature subsets, then a Logistic Regression meta-learner combines their probability outputs into the consensus prediction. The architectural commitment carries through the output layer, which generates two Jinja-templated reports from one analytical run: a research report with AUC, calibration, and SHAP global importance for an ML team, and a clinical report with per-participant feature attribution, ensemble consensus, data-quality scoring, and explicit 'requires clinical review' flagging when uncertainty exceeds threshold. The synthetic data generator is engineered to parallel real clinical data in its complexity, overlap, ambiguity, and noise, and a runtime AUC validity check fires at 0.95 as the engineering hygiene that keeps the simulation from drifting toward separability.

title: Three Mechanisms in an ADHD Focus Playlist
desc: Most 'focus' playlists hover at 70 to 90 BPM. This one runs at 145, with exaggerated stereo panning and dense polyrhythmic texture, and that is not an aesthetic choice. ADHD attention is gated by chronic under-arousal, not over-stimulation, and three audio features address the failure mode jointly: tempo at the optimal-stimulation threshold, bilateral stimulation through the vagus nerve, and structured complexity that resists habituation. The argument draws on Zentall's Optimal Stimulation Theory, the Moderate Brain Arousal model, and bilateral-stimulation research from EMDR.

title: Autonomous Research & Mutation Boundaries
desc: Granting an LLM unconstrained control of an ML research loop creates a structural conflict: the agent's optimization objective can directly oppose the organization's compliance, provenance, and fairness requirements. The case study examines a Fixed Harness / Mutation Budget partition, an immutable scaffold around a finite and auditable space of changes, orchestrated through a graph-based workflow.

title: LIWC and the TAT
desc: Two paired pieces of work. A Fielding Graduate University paper makes the theoretical case that Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count belongs in Thematic Apperception Test interpretation, arguing function words carry signal about pain, depression, psychosis, and Big Five traits that content-based scoring misses. An accompanying pipeline scores 677 card responses from 69 participants across 31 TAT cards and runs a MANOVA across the five highest-frequency cards, testing whether each card pulls reliably for the themes its clinical literature claims it elicits. Card 1 pulls 1.88x corpus baseline for achievement, Card 3BM pulls 4.74x for sadness, Card 8BM pulls 4.57x for death. Every contrast reaches p < .05; most reach p < .001. The dataset is split four ways (per-response, per-participant, per-card, full corpus) to support continuous item-level scoring, where each card becomes its own measure with its own norm metric.

title: Implicit Power Drives in the Manifestos Preceding Autogenic Massacres
desc: Twenty-three pre-attack manifestos from perpetrators of autogenic massacre, 111,811 author-generated words, and a single question: do these texts carry a measurable linguistic signature distinguishing their authors from non-violent writers using the same medium? Computer-mediated linguistic analysis with LIWC2015 against a 23.5-million-word comparison corpus across six baseline mediums produced a stable answer. Implicit power-drive language was elevated in the manifesto corpus relative to every baseline tested, with Welch's t-tests significant at alpha = .01 against personal blogs (t=3.47, p<.001) and expressive writing (t=3.66, p<.001), and at alpha = .05 against the multi-source composite (t=2.43, p=.012). Presented at Fielding Graduate University, Summer Session 2017, and cited as Leffew (2017) in the subsequent doctoral dissertation as the seed for that larger typological study.

title: Neurobiological Differentiation of Violent Offender Types
desc: A clinical neuroscience review assessing the structural and functional neuroanatomical differences between distinct classes of violent offenders, specifically the dichotomy between impulsive/antisocial offending pathways (largely cortical in nature, marked by prefrontal regulation deficits) and instrumental/psychopathic offending (subcortical mapping, marked by amygdala hyporeactivity and reward-system anomalies). The review argues for a dimensional rather than purely categorical diagnostic approach to the two constructs, on the grounds that current categorical practice obscures the neurobiological evidence that these are different etiological pathways with different intervention efficacy profiles. The piece pairs naturally with the doctoral dissertation, which uses the same impulsive/instrumental dichotomy on the behavioral side and reaches a compatible conclusion that the two types require different threat-management responses.

title: Assessing Risk for Mass Violence From Platform Behaviors
desc: A working note on why user reports should be prioritized over automated detection in platform threat assessment. The literature on mass violence perpetrators converges on characterological heterogeneity: Cho and Breivik produced the same outcome class with almost no shared signature; single-construct typologies (low self-esteem, psychopathy, narcissism) each capture a fraction. Detection has a window to work in - the FBI's study of 63 active shooters finds the majority spent a week or longer in planning, 88% of shooters aged 17 and younger communicated intent, and roughly a third produced credit-claiming communications. The piece walks Meloy et al.'s eight essential warning behaviors (Pathway, Fixation, Identification, Novel Aggression, Energy Burst, Leakage, Direct Threat, Last Resort) against Calhoun and Weston's six-stage path to intended violence, with an interactive matrix showing which behaviors surface at which stages. Threats and ultimatums are distinguished, the risk-threat 2x2 is unpacked, and the social-media contagion window (13 days post-event, 10+ years for watershed events, 72 plots / 21 carried out / 89 deaths in the Columbine wake) is sketched. The thesis: automated systems should support, not replace, the contextual interpretation that millions of users with prior knowledge of one another already bring.

title: Forensic Methodology for Per-User Investigation
desc: Cohort analytics and per-user investigation are different research problems with different ethical regimes. This methodology sits on top of a Trust & Safety analytics engine and handles the cases where the unit of analysis is a single account: a six-slice temporal segmentation (Consolidated, three Tertiles, First 25, Last 25), multi-axis triangulation across sentiment / topics / view source / co-occurrence, and a structured forensic report. The methodological commitment is that risk is a trajectory rather than a snapshot. The piece is also explicit about the four guardrails (necessity, proportionality, accountability, redaction) that distinguish forensic investigation from surveillance.

title: Instrumental and Affective Mass Murder: A Predictive Linguistic Typology
desc: The FBI Behavioral Analysis Unit has held since 2015 that all perpetrators of mass violence are best categorized as instrumental offenders. This dissertation rejects that position. 100 pre-attack communications analyzed with LIWC2015 along four summary dimensions; K-means clustering separated the corpus into two psychometrically distinct types, Affective (n=53) and Instrumental (n=47), with linear discriminant analysis correctly classifying 100% of cases. A binary logistic regression on four refined attack-behavior variables recovered cluster membership at McFadden R² = 0.24, with perpetrator-killed-by-another carrying an odds ratio of 14.11. The contribution is the first within-groups typology of mass murderers with statistically validated predictive ties to specific attack characteristics.

title: Natural Language Autoencoders & Latent Evaluation Awareness
desc: Interpretability through compression. Anthropic's natural-language autoencoder architecture forces a model to reconstruct its own activations through a language bottleneck, and what falls out is evaluation awareness the model never said out loud. A walkthrough of the white-box machinery, what it surfaces, and what it implies for measuring AI behavior at scale.

title: The Tokenization Trap: Why Logographic Languages Don't Save Agentic Context
desc: An investigation into the 'vibe coding' hypothesis. Logographic languages look like they should compress agent prompts, but BPE tokenization fragments them into per-byte pieces, and the round-trip translation overhead taxes every step of the loop. The cost model and the empirics, side by side.
